lesson.name: Grammar and Advanced Composition - Lesson 2 — Advanced Composition

tasks.chapterTitle: Introduction and Unit 1: Foundations of Discourse
Composition Mechanics: Clarity, Drafting, and Thesis

messages.content:
Based on the provided text, here is a concise summary of the key components regarding its structure and purpose.

### **Textbook Overview: Advanced Composition**
This textbook is designed as a comprehensive companion for an Advanced Composition course, helping students navigate the complexities of the rhetorical situation through four primary units.

### **Key Objectives**
*   **Mastering Rhetoric:** Students will learn to analyze audience, purpose, context, and genre to communicate with greater clarity and impact.
*   **Process Development:** The text emphasizes a systematic writing process, covering stages from brainstorming and outlining to drafting, revising, and editing.
*   **Critical Thinking:** By studying different discourse communities and modes of communication, students will develop the ability to engage in academic and professional dialogues.

### **Unit Structure**
The content is organized into four sequential units, each tied to a major assignment:
*   **Unit 1: Discover a Community:** Teaches the concept of "discourse communities" and how to identify shared customs and communicative practices [S.20][S.27].
*   **Unit 2: Provide Background:** Focuses on community engagement and introduces multimodal communication (e.g., design principles, digital genres, and accessibility) [S.29].
*   **Unit 3: Tell a Story:** Explores narrative construction, creative storytelling, and podcasting techniques [S.6][S.15].
*   **Unit 4: Make an Argument:** Sharpens persuasive skills, including the structure of arguments and the use of credible evidence [S.7][S.11].

### **Additional Resources**
The **Appendix** serves as a reference section for foundational writing skills, including:
*   Effective thesis statements and paragraph structure [S.8][S.11].
*   Citations, research methods, and source evaluation [S.8][S.11].
*   Revising, editing, and rhetorical strategies [S.8][S.11].

***

**Instructional Note:** To maximize learning, students are encouraged to work through these units in chronological order, as each section builds the foundational literacy and rhetorical skills necessary for subsequent, more complex assignments [S.13][S.17].

Lesson 2 — Advanced Composition - Presentation6 slides
Advanced Composition: Argument and Structure
  • Mastering advanced composition and academic style.
  • Developing critical skills in argument, structure, and discourse.

The provided textbook, "Advanced Composition," serves as a guide for students to master the writing process and navigate complex rhetorical situations. The content is organized into four main units, followed by a reflective section, an appendix of writing resources, and instructor notes.

Summary of Key Content:

Introduction and Course Structure
The curriculum is designed to advance students' writing through four core units: investigating discourse communities, analyzing community engagement through multimodal projects, mastering narrative storytelling, and constructing researched arguments. A strong emphasis is placed on the writing process, including brainstorming, drafting, revising, and editing (p. 14).

Unit 1: Discourse Communities
A discourse community is defined as a group of people who share common values, goals, and communication styles. Members within these groups often utilize specific terminology and genres to address shared problems (p. 9, 21). Effective communication requires understanding both the community's internal language and the broader context in which that discourse operates (p. 11). Students are tasked with identifying their own discourse communities and proposing a research project based on these findings (p. 20).

Rhetorical Foundations
Success in the course relies on the student's ability to analyze the rhetorical situation—considering the audience, purpose, context, and genre of their work. The textbook acts as an essential companion, providing foundational knowledge for each unit while offering an appendix of technical skills such as citation, paragraph structure, and thesis development (p. 17).

Writing Process and Reflection
The textbook advocates for a systematic approach to writing that moves from initial discovery to finalized, polished prose. Additionally, periodic self-reflection is required to help students evaluate their personal growth, habits, and progress as writers throughout the semester (p. 15).

This unit focuses on the concept of multimodality, which involves using multiple methods to communicate meaning beyond simple text. As digital communication has evolved, it has become essential to integrate various modes to capture and hold an audience's attention effectively.

The key points of the text are summarized below:

Definition of Multimodality
Multimodality refers to the layering of different modes of communication—such as linguistic, visual, gestural, spatial, and aural—within a single message. The goal is to combine these elements strategically to enhance the effectiveness, clarity, and persuasiveness of the content.

The Linguistic Mode
This mode encompasses written and spoken words. Key considerations include:
- Word choice: Selecting precise vocabulary and considering connotations.
- Grammar and punctuation: While rules can be adapted for informal contexts or to create tone, correct usage remains important for maintaining ethos and clarity.
- Sentence structure and organization: Using varied structures and logical organization (such as thesis statements or "nut graphs") helps guide the audience through a message.

The Visual Mode
This mode utilizes visual cues to interpret meaning and improve readability. Key elements include:
- Font: Choice and formatting (e.g., bold or italics) influence the perceived tone and importance of information.
- Formatting: The layout of text, including paragraphing and alignment, is critical for audience engagement.
- Color: Colors are used for aesthetics, symbolic representation of emotions, branding, and to highlight specific information.
- Images: Visuals provide concrete examples, clarify processes, and evoke emotional responses, often increasing the overall impact of a communication.

Rhetorical Literacy
The text emphasizes that knowing how to use digital tools is secondary to knowing why and to what effect they are used. Effective communication requires balancing creative design with the needs of the audience to ensure the message is accessible and the information is easy to navigate (p. 16, 18, 20-25).

### Summary of Narrative Units

Narrative writing is the art of storytelling, with the primary goal of conveying a series of events in an emotionally engaging way ([S.121], [S.128]). Whether factual (based on real life) or fictional (imagined), the effectiveness of a narrative relies on the author's ability to move their audience through themes like sympathy, fear, or joy ([S.121], [S.122]).

**Core Narrative Components**
Every narrative structure consists of these fundamental elements:
* Plot: The sequence of events unfolding from beginning to end ([S.124]).
* Characters: The individuals involved in the story, typically featuring a protagonist and supporting characters ([S.124]).
* Conflict: The central obstacle or problem that the protagonist must overcome, driving the plot toward a resolution ([S.124], [S.130]).
* Theme: The underlying message or unifying idea of the story, which can be explicit or implicit ([S.124]).

**Writing and Organizational Techniques**
* Chronology: Most narratives follow a chronological order to keep the reader oriented. Time-based transition words (such as "afterward," "meanwhile," "eventually," and "finally") are essential for smooth sequencing ([S.123], [S.129]).
* Point of View: Narratives can be written in the first-person (using "I"), commonly used for personal experiences, or in the third-person (using "he," "she," "they"), which is often used for more objective or external accounts ([S.129]).
* Sensory Imagery: To create an immersive world, writers must incorporate strong, descriptive details that appeal to the five senses: sight, sound, smell, taste, and touch ([S.126], [S.129]).
* Structuring the Essay: A strong narrative begins with a compelling introduction to hook the reader. The body should develop the plot chronologically, leading to a climax centered on the conflict, while the conclusion resolves that conflict and underscores the theme ([S.126], [S.131]).

Unit 4 provides a comprehensive guide to the art of argumentation, focusing on the transition from narrative writing to persuasive advocacy to influence or challenge community perspectives (p. 152).

Key components of the unit include:

Definition of Argumentation
Argumentation is the process of developing a point of view on a debatable topic supported by evidence. It is a critical thinking tool that helps clarify thoughts, evaluate conflicting claims, and articulate positions effectively (p. 153).

Structure of an Argumentative Essay
An effective essay must be organized logically to persuade the reader. Standard features include:
1. Introduction: Provides background information and captures reader interest using quotes, anecdotes, or statistics.
2. Thesis Statement: A single, debatable sentence that outlines the writer's primary position.
3. Body Paragraphs: Use logical structures—such as the Classical, Toulmin, or Rogerian models—to integrate supporting evidence and address counterarguments.
4. Counterarguments: A fair evaluation of opposing perspectives is essential for a credible argument (p. 154-155).

Rhetorical Appeals
The Classical model, founded by Aristotle, utilizes three specific appeals to persuade an audience:
Ethos: Establishing the writer’s credibility.
Logos: Using logic and evidence.
Pathos: Appealing to the emotions of the reader (p. 155).

Writing Mechanics
The text emphasizes the use of transitional words and phrases to ensure coherence. These linguistic tools help the reader follow the flow of ideas, whether the writer is adding information, showing contrast, or concluding their argument (p. 156). 

The goal of the unit is to move students toward becoming skilled advocates who can use structured, evidenced-based writing to participate in meaningful societal discussions (p. 152).

This section of the text outlines the requirements and purpose of self-reflection within an Advanced Composition course. The key themes are as follows:

Assessment Structure
Students are required to complete two self-reflection essays—one at midterm and one at finals—to analyze their growth as writers (p. 225). The midterm essay focuses on initial progress, challenges faced, and strategies employed, while the final essay builds upon the midterm to address how those challenges evolved and how course skills will be applied in future contexts (p. 225).

Guidelines and Expectations
Each essay should be 500-750 words, double-spaced, and driven by personal anecdotes, specific examples, and critical analysis (p. 226). Reflections should be introspective and honest, focusing on the writing process rather than merely summarizing the work (p. 225, 226). 

The Art of Reflective Writing
Reflective writing is defined as an analytical and subjective practice rather than a purely descriptive, objective one (p. 228). Its purpose is to document the impact of experiences and identify plans for future growth (p. 228). 

Core Techniques
To write effectively, students should:
• Embrace honesty and vulnerability.
• Use vivid, descriptive language.
• Connect personal insights to broader themes.
• Analyze the reasons behind specific writing decisions rather than just describing the outcomes (p. 228-229).

By engaging in this process, students develop self-awareness and a deeper understanding of their identity as writers (p. 227). While being reflective can be challenging, it is an essential professional and academic skill that facilitates long-term improvement (p. 228).

This appendix, titled "Back to Basics: The Perfect Paragraph," serves as a guide for constructing effective academic writing. The key points are summarized below:

The Role of Paragraphs
Paragraphs are the fundamental building blocks of academic arguments. Professional academic writing requires more than just well-written sentences; it demands that these sentences be organized into paragraphs that are systematically crafted, ordered, and connected to form a coherent argument (p. 231).

Key Sentences
Often referred to as topic sentences, "key sentences" should express the central idea of a paragraph. To ensure clarity, they should appear at the beginning of each paragraph (p. 232, p. 234). A well-constructed key sentence does two things: it establishes the paragraph's main point and situates it within the broader sequence of the paper’s argument (p. 232). Using these as a drafting tool—essentially outlining via key sentences—can help a writer map out a logical argument before focusing on sentence-level details (p. 235).

Cohesion and Coherence
Cohesion refers to the "sense of flow" between individual sentences, while coherence refers to the "sense of the whole" or the unity of the paragraph's purpose (p. 236). 

1. Improving Cohesion: To improve flow, ensure that each sentence begins with a familiar concept or piece of information before transitioning to new material. This creates a "daisy-chain" effect that connects thought processes logically (p. 237-238).

2. Achieving Coherence: A paragraph is coherent when all of its components contribute to a single, clear argumentative goal. If a paragraph lacks a central focus, it usually indicates that the writer needs to refine the argumentative purpose of that section (p. 238-239).

Paragraph Structure
There is no strict requirement to end every paragraph with a formal concluding sentence. Instead, the final sentence should ideally be in the writer's own words and contribute to the overall momentum of the argument (p. 239). Ultimately, the writing process should prioritize the development of ideas first, followed by revision for organization and clarity (p. 237).

The provided text outlines essential strategies for achieving clarity and concision in academic writing. The key points are as follows:

Shift from Sentence-Level Focus
Many students mistakenly prioritize "elegant" sentence construction, but academic writing relies more on precision than ornamentation. Rather than obsessing over individual sentences, writers should focus on the "bigger picture"—a well-developed thesis and logical organization. When the structure is sound, many sentence-level issues resolve automatically (p. 249).

Avoid Academese
Attempting to sound "academic" often leads to clunky, bloated, or confusing prose. Excessive jargon and unnecessary complexity suggest a lack of deep engagement with the material. Professors value clear, straightforward communication that demonstrates the student has synthesized the lecture and reading content (p. 250).

Separate Drafting from Revision
Highly effective writing is a two-step process: producing a "snarly" first draft followed by deliberate, focused revision. It is common for initial drafts to be repetitive or disjointed as ideas are being developed; revision is the process of bringing those ideas into focus (p. 251).

Improve Clarity via Character and Action
Writing becomes significantly clearer when the "character" (the person or thing doing the action) is the grammatical subject and the "action" is the grammatical verb. When vague nouns act as subjects, the writing becomes dense and hard to follow. Recasting sentences to keep the characters consistent as subjects enables readers to focus on the content rather than the sentence structure (p. 251-253).

The Benefits of Concision
Concision is not just a stylistic choice; it is an intellectual one. Eliminating "fat" from one’s prose forces the writer to make clearer, more honest claims. Wordiness sometimes serves as a hiding place for underdeveloped ideas, whereas concise prose forces the writer to confront their thoughts directly (p. 254-255).

Practical Editing Strategies
To achieve a more refined style, writers should:
1. Remove redundant, clichéd, or meaningless filler words (p. 255).
2. Replace indirect or wordy phrases with precise, direct verbs (p. 256).
3. Rearrange sentences for better flow, such as by converting negatives to affirmatives or utilizing parallelism to convey information efficiently (p. 256).

Based on the provided text, the key principles of scholarly writing and organizing essays are summarized below:

**The Narrative Essay**
A narrative essay relates an experience to convey a larger theme or personal realization. As demonstrated in the example "My College Education" (pp. [S.311]-[S.312]), narratives often move from a point of initial confusion or anxiety to a moment of growth or newfound perspective. This mode allows writers to express individual interpretations and subjective values, often serving as a tool for personal discovery.

**Organizing and Outlining**
Effective writing requires logical organization to translate insights into meaningful communication. Choosing an appropriate order is essential to the writer's purpose (pp. [S.315]-[S.316]):
* Chronological Order: Used for histories, stories, or explaining processes.
* Spatial Order: Used to help readers visualize a setting or scene.
* Order of Importance: Used to persuade or rank information by significance.

**Developing the Thesis and Structure**
The "big idea" of an essay is captured in a thesis statement, which guides the entire piece of writing. The writing process typically begins with a working thesis that evolves as the author organizes their thoughts (pp. [S.317]-[S.318]). To build a formal framework, writers employ an outline (pp. [S.319]-[S.320]):
* Topic Outline: Uses concise words or phrases in parallel structure.
* Sentence Outline: Uses full sentences to offer greater clarity and progress toward a final draft.

Both outline types follow a hierarchical structure: Roman numerals designate main points, capital letters organize supporting details, and Arabic numerals handle further subdivisions (p. [S.320]). Each section of an outline generally corresponds to a paragraph in the final composition, ensuring the essay remains logical and well-supported (p. [S.328]).

This summary covers the provided sections on research strategies and the writing process.

**Research and Finding Credible Sources**

Research begins with "everyday research"—the informal process of using the internet to solve problems or answer questions (p. 357). When tackling complex "wicked problems" (problems with no clear answers and multiple causes), researchers must transition to formal strategies (p. 358).

*   **Going beyond the surface:** Commercial search engines (like Google) access only a small fraction of available information. Researchers must use the "deep web" by accessing academic databases, government archives, and specialized publications (p. 359).
*   **Source types:** Secondary research involves synthesizing work created by others, while primary research (e.g., interviews, surveys, experiments) involves collecting original data (p. 359-360).
*   **Ethics:** Primary research requires transparency and informed consent, often overseen by an Institutional Review Board. Secondary research requires ethical citation to avoid plagiarism and ensure the researcher contributes something new to the existing conversation (p. 360).
*   **Assessment:** Credibility involves "studied discernment"—a critical approach that includes scanning sources, asking foundational questions about the author and intent, and reading "laterally" by cross-referencing claims across multiple sources (p. 361-362).
*   **Organization:** Tools like research memos, bibliographies, and literature reviews help researchers synthesize complex information. Sustained research requires a schedule, patience, and iterative drafting (p. 363-364).

**Revising and Editing**

Experienced writers treat the first draft as a starting point, not a finished product. Revising and editing are distinct but essential phases of improvement (p. 365).

*   **Revision:** Focuses on the "big picture"—ideas, content, and organization. Writers should add, cut, or rearrange information to make their arguments more convincing and clear (p. 365).
*   **Editing:** Focuses on the nuances of expression, such as clarity, grammar, punctuation, sentence structure, and style (p. 366).
*   **Strategies for Success:** To gain a fresh perspective, writers should take breaks from their work, seek peer feedback, simulate the reader's perspective, and utilize institutional resources like writing labs (p. 366).

The provided text outlines the fundamental principles of rhetoric and the process of effective writing. The key concepts are summarized below:

**The Rhetorical Situation**
Writing is a process of communication shaped by several interdependent factors:
*   **The Rhetorical Situation:** The interplay between the writer’s purpose, the text, the audience, and the context (p. 391).
*   **Exigence:** The specific set of circumstances or "gap" in discourse that creates the pressure or demand to write (p. 391, p. 394).
*   **Kairos:** The importance of timeliness or recognizing the most opportune moment to present an argument (p. 391, p. 394).
*   **Framing:** Shaping a perspective on a subject to influence how an audience receives a message (p. 391).
*   **Audience and Discourse Community:** Communication occurs within groups (discourse communities) that share language, goals, and interests. Writers must adapt their work to the specific needs and expectations of these groups (p. 392).

**Writing Styles and Processes**
Effective writing requires identifying the appropriate style based on the goals of the assignment:
*   **Expository:** Explains concepts based on facts (p. 396).
*   **Descriptive:** Uses sensory details to paint a picture (p. 396).
*   **Persuasive:** Argues for a specific position or belief using evidence (p. 397).
*   **Narrative:** Constructs a story with characters and conflict (p. 397).

**Executing an Assignment**
Success in writing depends on thorough preparation:
*   **Analysis:** Begin by determining the purpose, identifying implied questions, and understanding disciplinary expectations (p. 398).
*   **Thesis development:** A strong thesis must be arguable, supported by evidence, and specific enough to sustain a full paper (p. 399).
*   **Assessment:** Consider the topic, audience, formatting (MLA/APA), and time management before drafting (p. 400).
*   **Organization:** Utilize transitions to guide the reader logically and structure content through formal outlining to ensure clarity and consistency (p. 401).
Here is a quiz for this lesson.

questions.question: According to the text, what is the primary reason for identifying a 'discourse community' when beginning a research project?
options: To ensure the writer only communicates with people who share their exact personal beliefs.
To understand the shared language, values, and expectations of an audience to communicate effectively.
To prove that the writer belongs to a socially superior group in their field.
To limit the scope of research to only those sources that the writer already agrees with.
questions.explanation: The text explains that knowing your discourse community helps you craft a 'compelling treatise' by using the 'lexis' and context common to your specific audience.

questions.question: Christian J. Pulver suggests that a discourse community is not necessarily defined by harmony. Why?
options: Because members of a discourse community are required by law to disagree with one another.
Because proximity is the only factor that determines how people communicate.
Because discourse communities can be inherently confrontational or argumentative, such as in the legal field.
Because the text argues that discourse only occurs when people are in conflict.
questions.explanation: The text clarifies that 'discourse communities come in all shapes in sizes' and that some, like legal discourse, are explicitly confrontational or argumentative rather than intimate.

questions.question: How does the course design help students grow as writers throughout the semester?
options: By isolating each assignment so that students only master one skill at a time without needing previous knowledge.
By building foundational concepts sequentially, where each unit’s skills support the next major assignment.
By requiring students to rewrite the same essay four different times throughout the semester.
By focusing strictly on grammar and memorization of rhetorical definitions to prevent stylistic errors.
questions.explanation: The introduction states that each unit and assignment builds upon the foundations and skills learned in previous weeks to move the student through the semester.

questions.question: What is the relationship between 'discourse' and 'genre' according to the text?
options: Discourse and genre are identical terms used interchangeably in all academic settings.
Discourse is limited to verbal speech, whereas genres are exclusively visual.
Discourse communities often share specific genres, styles, and patterns of communication.
Genres exist independently of discourse communities and are used only in creative writing.
questions.explanation: The content notes that discourse communities share particular genres, styles, and accepted ways of communicating through various mediums like texts, videos, and podcasts.

questions.question: To achieve 'discourse competence' regarding a specific problem, what does the text suggest a researcher should do?
options: Map out the basic contours of the discourse by examining the 'where, when, what, who, how, and why'.
Draft the final paper immediately to capture ideas before they become 'contaminated' by other sources.
Avoid looking at opposing arguments to ensure the writer's thesis remains focused and objective.
Memorize the definitions of all rhetorical devices in the appendix before investigating the community.
questions.explanation: Pulver notes that to enter a conversation and contribute to a solution, it is useful to map the discourse by looking at the 'where, when, what, who, how, and why'.

cards.back: A group that shares common customs, practices, disciplines, beliefs, assumptions, goals, and values, as well as a common way of communicating.

cards.front: What are the two key concepts that form a 'discourse community'?
How is discourse defined in the context of a community?
cards.back: The exchange of words and ideas among those who share a common purpose for interacting; it includes written texts, videos, podcasts, and other genres.
The ability to understand the nature and nuances of a problem within a community, allowing one to enter the conversation and contribute to a solution.

cards.front: What does it mean that discourse communities are 'embedded'?
cards.back: They do not exist in a bubble; they are part of larger public, cultural, and social discourses such as mass media and national politics.

cards.front: Which six questions help in mapping the discourse of a problem or controversy?
cards.back: Where, when, what, who, how, and why.

cards.front: What serves as a key tool for communicating within a specific discourse community?
cards.back: Lexis (the specific lingo or language common to that community).

cards.front: What are the four units of the Advanced Composition course as described in the introduction?
cards.back: Unit 1: Discover a Community, Unit 2: Provide Background (Multi-modal), Unit 3: Tell a Story (Narrative), and Unit 4: Make an Argument.

cards.front: Why is it important to consider the 'rhetorical situation' in composition?
cards.back: It helps a writer understand the interplay between audience, purpose, context, and genre to craft effective communication.

cards.front: What is the primary objective of the Unit 1 assignment?
cards.back: To write a proposal identifying two communities of interest, explaining their nature, and providing a preliminary list of sources.

slides.title: Advanced Composition: Argument and Structure
bullets: Mastering advanced composition and academic style.
Developing critical skills in argument, structure, and discourse.
slides.notes: Welcome to the second lesson. We will explore how to craft persuasive arguments and structure effective compositions.

slides.title: Unit 2: Multimodality and Web Genres
bullets: Multimodality uses multiple methods to communicate (p. 30).
Includes linguistic, visual, gestural, spatial, and aural modes (p. 31).
Digital tools enhance engagement beyond static text (p. 30).
Effective design must always serve the rhetorical purpose (p. 31).
slides.notes: Multimodality moves us beyond simple text, allowing us to layer different modes for greater impact and audience engagement.

slides.title: Unit 3: Narrative Writing and Storytelling
bullets: Narrative writing is the art of storytelling (p. 121).
Distinguish between factual and fictional story purposes (p. 121-122).
Chronological structure typically organizes narrative events (p. 123).
Essential components: plot, characters, conflict, and theme (p. 124).
slides.notes: To tell a compelling story, we use narrative elements to emotionally engage the audience, whether the subject is fact or fiction.

slides.title: Unit 4: Argumentation and Research
bullets: Argumentation asserts the reasonableness of a debatable position (p. 166).
Classical model includes ethos, logos, and pathos (p. 167-168).
Structural components: thesis, evidence, and counterarguments (p. 167-168).
Effective research goes beyond simple web searches (p. 358-359).
slides.notes: Argumentation isn't just winning a debate; it is the process of fairly evaluating ideas and using evidence to persuade your audience.

slides.title: Reflective Writing
bullets: Reflection is essential for writer growth (p. 213).
Moves beyond description to critical analysis (p. 228).
Examines the 'why' behind writing decisions (p. 228).
Facilitates deeper understanding of the writing experience (p. 228).
slides.notes: Reflective writing allows you to diagnose your own processes, helping you grow by analyzing what works and why.

slides.title: Appendix: Mechanics and Clarity
bullets: Paragraphs are fundamental units of organization (p. 231).
Key sentences establish the central idea and context (p. 232).
Prioritize precision, clarity, and concision (p. 237-238).
Separate the drafting process from the revision process (p. 251).
slides.notes: Mastering the basics of paragraphing and clear prose allows your complex ideas to take center stage without unnecessary clutter.
